summary: Joel criticizes Microsoft's corporate communication style, specifically their announcement about SQL Server 2008 delays. Instead of simply saying the product would be late, Microsoft issued a jargon-filled statement full of meaningless phrases like 'feature complete CTP during the Heroes Happen Here launch wave.' Joel argues the announcement is deliberately obfuscatory and calls on the author to just be honest. He notes that nobody would have been upset by a straightforward delay announcement since SQL Server 2005 works fine.
key_points: Microsoft used impenetrable corporate jargon to disguise the fact that SQL Server 2008 was delayed
Honest, direct communication about delays is always better than obfuscation, especially when customers don't care that much
Corporate-speak erodes trust more than the bad news it tries to hide
money_quotes: Have some guts. Just say it's late.
We really don't care that much. SQL Server 2005 is fine.
A feature complete CTP during the Heroes Happen Here launch wave? What on earth does that mean?
Don't piss on my leg and tell me it's raining.
key_insight: Companies destroy trust not by delivering bad news, but by burying it under corporate jargon instead of communicating honestly.
